title: Chapter 11 Quiz
description: Test your File I/O skills and your mastery of the brilliant Enumerable module!

questions.question: What happens to your variables naturally when a Ruby script finishes execution?
options: They stay in memory for the next run.
They are instantly lost forever unless you write them to a hard drive file.
questions.explanation: RAM (memory) is temporary. Hard Drives (Files) are permanent.

questions.question: What is the global built-in Ruby class dedicated entirely to hard drive interaction?
questions.explanation: The `File` class contains every method you need to touch the physical disk.

questions.question: Which mode string correctly tells Ruby to open a file in 'read-only' mode?
questions.explanation: The "r" stands specifically for Read.

questions.question: If you manually open a file using `File.open("data.txt", "r")`, you MUST explicitly call `file.close` when you are done.
questions.explanation: If you don't use a block, failing to close a file creates identical memory leaks to a stuck download.

questions.question: Why is utilizing a `do...end` Block the universally preferred way to open manipulate files?
options: It instantly flawlessy automatically closes the file the nanosecond the block ends.
questions.explanation: Blocks completely eliminate human error regarding `.close`.

questions.question: DANGER! What violently happens if you open an existing 500-line file explicitly using ONLY `"w"` mode?
options: An error is thrown to protect it.
Your new text is added to line 501.
Ruby instantly erases all 500 lines to give you a pristine blank canvas.
It opens in read mode instead.
questions.explanation: Write ("w") explicitly completely overwrites.

questions.question: Which mode properly safely adds your new line of text directly to the very bottom of an existing file?
questions.explanation: "a" stands for 'Append', completely preserving all history.

questions.question: Arrays and Hashes amazingly flawlessly share exactly identical iteration methods (like `.each`). How?
options: They inherit from the same parent.
They both secretly `include` the massive `Enumerable` Module.
They are the exact same class.
questions.explanation: The Enumerable Mixin is the beating heart of Ruby data manipulation.

questions.question: If you build a Custom Class and want to manually `include Enumerable`, what is the ONE STRICT REQUIREMENT?
options: Your class must be an Array.
Your class must have an `initialize` method.
Your class MUST explicitly define an `each` method that mathematically yields its items.
questions.explanation: The entire Enumerable module fundamentally relies on your `each` method as its core engine.

questions.question: What is the extreme functional difference between `.each` and `.map`?
options: `.each` is for Hashes. `.map` is for Arrays.
`.each` loops blindly blindly. `.map` captures the returning block values and brilliantly builds a brand new transformed array.
questions.explanation: If you want to beautifully fundamentally TRANSFORM an array of data into a new array, you use `.map`.

questions.question: What does appending an exclamation mark (a 'bang' -> `!`) generally mean on Ruby methods like `.map!`?
options: It makes the method execute asynchronously.
It violently permanently mutates (destroys) the original object data instead of safely generating a new copy.
questions.explanation: The bang `!` universally signals 'Warning! I am about to physically alter the raw memory of this variable definitively!'

questions.question: Which Enumerable method specifically cleanly iterates an array and meticulously accurately produces a new array strictly containing *only* the elements that returned `true`?
questions.explanation: Select is the ultimate filtering tool. If TRUE, the item effortlessly stays.

questions.question: Which method performs mathematically the exact opposite of `.select`?
questions.explanation: Reject safely uniquely creates a brand new array filled exclusively with any objects that aggressively returned FALSE.

questions.question: What is an alias for the `.map` method that does the exact perfectly same thing?
questions.explanation: `.map` and `.collect` are synonymous.

questions.question: If `File.open("test.txt", "w")` is called but the file actually really truly doesn't exist on the hard drive yet, what intelligently happens?
options: Ruby instantly efficiently automatically creates a brand new blank physical file for you.
It asks the user to make it.
questions.explanation: Write mode guarantees the file exists before writing, even if it has to invent it.

questions.question: What specifically gets written if you run `file.puts "Hello"` in write mode inside a block?
options: The string 'Hello' seamlessly appended to the terminal.
The string 'Hello' smoothly followed by an absolute new line directly into the hard drive file.
The string 'Hello' directly into the memory exclusively.
questions.explanation: `puts` seamlessly acts identically to how it does in the terminal, just perfectly rerouted entirely into the physical file.

questions.question: Consider `[1,2].map { |n| n + 1 }`. What does this perfectly identically evaluate to?
questions.explanation: Map specifically safely transforms every element flawlessly.

questions.question: Consider `[1,2,3].select { |n| n > 2 }`. What does this flawlessly predictably evaluate to?
questions.explanation: Only the integer `3` cleanly safely passes the boolean greater-than condition.

questions.question: Consider `[1,2,3].reject { |n| n == 2 }`. What does this evaluate to?
questions.explanation: It perfectly explicitly deletes uniquely any element matching exactly 2.

questions.question: What is fundamentally visually unique about Enumerable methods strictly explicitly compared to standard loops like `while`?
options: Enumerable methods are exclusively always called brilliantly elegantly directly onto an Object using heavily utilizing `.dot` notation followed purely by a Block.
Enumerable methods strictly never accurately use Blocks.
questions.explanation: Because they are strictly precisely Object Methods, you chain them seamlessly natively onto variables smoothly: `array.map { }`.
